business_ideas.idea: A credentialing/rating agency (like JD Power or Mensa) that charges companies or individuals for a 'trust' stamp or score
A standardized athletic combine-style testing service for youth/amateur athletes (like the 'SPARQ' test), monetized via travel-sports parents
A niche 'aptitude score' for hobbies like chess (e.g. a paid 'Chess IQ' test for students)
Buying or partnering with OrgOrg (a volunteer-run EA/office-admin community) to build a staffing/reputation platform
A 'Lambda School for executive assistants/secretaries' — a 3-month training program funded via income-share agreements, placing grads into $50-80K EA jobs
Sam's List: a personally curated, vetted directory of vendors (copywriters, law firms, accountants) for founders
Cryptographic tamper-proof 'legitimacy seals' baked into phone cameras to combat deepfake video/audio fraud

predictions.text: Deepfakes will make fully AI-generated actors (like a fake 'Brad Pitt') possible in movies.
Deepfake/fake-news problems will get much worse before device makers implement tamper-proof capture verification.
We're 6-8 more weeks from returning to normal after COVID lockdowns (said in jest).

dynamics: Both co-hosts (Sam Parr and Shaan Puri) are present together, recording remotely during COVID lockdown; energy is casual, joking, and fast-riffing, bouncing rapid-fire between unrelated topics (deepfakes, credentialing businesses, a hypothetical EA trade school, a billionaire profile) rather than following a single narrative. A brief guest cameo from podcaster Jordan Harbinger opens the episode to promote his show, distinct from the main hosts-only segment structure that follows — reflecting the show's shift toward a cohosted, segment-based format.
frameworks: Lambda School / income-share agreement model: free training funded by taking a cut of graduates' future salary, aligning incentives around placement outcomes
'Use your persuasion powers for good': the ethics of charisma/manipulation hinge on whether your underlying intent helps or exploits the other party
Learn technique from villains: study effective (even unethical) communicators like Hitler or Trump for tactics, while consciously repurposing them for good ends
learnings: Credentialing/rating businesses (JD Power, Mensa, StrengthsFinder) can be extremely profitable because they sell trust as a service with very low marginal cost once the brand is established.
Ratings agencies can lose credibility fast if the entities they rate also fund them (cited the 2008 mortgage-bond rating conflict of interest as a cautionary parallel).
Founders who diversify wildly into unrelated categories (Xavier Niel, Bernard Arnault's LVMH from a construction company) often only look coherent in hindsight, over a 50-100 year arc.

A staffing/community platform becomes self-reinforcing once you place enough candidates — your own alumni become referral sources and your brand becomes the trusted default, similar to the Lambda School flywheel.
Studying persuasive but unethical communicators can be instructive purely on technique (message simplicity, visual contrast, repetition) separate from endorsing their goals.

banger_quotes.quote: I hate working from home. I hate it so much.
My rule is this: if your intentions are good, then it's all fair game.
Email list is our pirate ship and every email is a little bit of wind in our sails.
This guy's my favorite person ever.

numbers_dropped: JD Power was sold to McGraw Hill in 2005 for $400 million, then resold in 2018 for over $1 billion
Mensa has about 150,000 members and does about $4.8 million a year in revenue
StrengthsFinder's parent company reportedly makes hundreds of millions to $1-2 billion a year from a $29-49 quiz
About 600,000 executive-assistant/secretary jobs exist in the US, paying roughly $50-80K
Xavier Niel's net worth is cited around $6.6 billion; he put $150M+ into building tuition-free coding schools ('42') in France and Fremont, CA
Xavier Niel sold an early stake (WorldNet) for $50 million right before the dot-com crash
Bernard Arnault's family sold their construction business for roughly $20 million (60s dollars) before pivoting into luxury goods and building LVMH
notable: Structurally distinct from the earlier single-host guest-interview era: both cohosts appear together in a segment-based format (a Jordan Harbinger cameo, a rapid idea-brainstorm section, and a recurring 'my new favorite rich guy' billionaire-profile segment), recorded remotely during COVID-19 lockdown.
